title: What Did I Lose When I Lost Them, Beyond Them
subtitle: A journal prompt set for naming the smaller losses inside the big one
excerpt: A journal prompt set for the losses inside the loss. The person is gone; so are the small daily things that came with them. Naming the smaller losses is part of the grieving. Used at any point in the long arc.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The secondary-losses frame is a staple of grief literature and grief work.

practice.whenToUse: Use at any point in the grief arc when the loss feels bigger than the person, or when you cannot name why the day has gone heavy.

content.text: A journal prompt set for the losses inside the loss. The person is gone. So are the small daily things that came with them: the phone calls, the meals, the way a room felt when they were in it. Naming the smaller losses is part of the grief.
New to journal prompts as a practice? Read
journal prompts as practice
first.
The practice
Warm-up
Who was this person to me, in one paragraph?
Write the short version. Not the eulogy. The truth as it sat with you. Three or four sentences.
What did the week look like with them in it?
Run a normal week in your head, the way it used to be. Where did they show up? What were the regular contacts? What was the rhythm?
Closer in
What did I lose in the daily routine?
Name the small things. The phone call on the way home. The Sunday lunch. The first person you would ring with good news. The person who would notice the new haircut.
What did I lose about who I was around them?
There are versions of us that only existed with certain people. The daughter version, the friend version, the work version. Name the version of you that only existed with them.
What did I lose that I have not let myself grieve yet?
Sometimes the secondary losses are the hardest to name because they feel small next to the main loss. They are not small. Write the one or two that you have been carrying without naming.
What is one small thing I can do this week to mark one of those losses?
One small act. A meal you used to share with them, made and eaten this week. A phone call to someone who knew them. A walk on a route that was theirs.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education. The secondary-losses frame is a staple of grief writing and of grief work.
